title: Four-shaft summer and winter weave
subtitle: Threading and treadling a reversible block pattern on a four-shaft loom
excerpt: Summer and winter is a traditional American and European weave structure that produces bold two-colour block patterns on a four-shaft loom. The cloth is reversible: where one colour shows on the face, the other shows on the back. This entry covers the threading, tie-up, and treadling for a two-block sample.
sourceNotes: Summer and winter weave is documented in Mary E. Black, The Key to Weaving (1945, in-copyright, substance only referenced), Mary Atwater, The Shuttle-Craft Book of American Hand-Weaving (1928, in copyright, substance only referenced), and in Hooper, Hand-Loom Weaving (1910, public domain, Internet Archive).

glossaryTerms.definition: The pattern by which each warp thread is assigned to a specific shaft. In summer and winter, each block uses two shafts: one ground shaft (shaft 1 or 2) and one pattern shaft (shaft 3 or 4).
The connection pattern between treadles and shafts. In summer and winter, the tie-up determines which colour appears on the face of each block.
The sequence in which the treadles are pressed to produce the weave pattern. Each treadle lifts the shafts tied to it.

A supplementary-weft weave structure on four shafts that produces reversible two-colour block patterns. The name reflects the two-sided nature of the cloth: one colour on the face, the other on the back.

content.text: Summer and winter is one of the most versatile four-shaft block weave structures. Each block of pattern is controlled by a pair of shafts (shafts 1+3 for block A, shafts 2+4 for block B), and the treadling alternates between two pattern picks and a ground pick. The cloth is reversible: where colour A dominates on the face, colour B dominates on the back.

items.name: Warp yarn, cotton 8/2 or linen 10/2, colour A (ground)
Pattern weft yarn, wool or cotton, colour B (pattern)
Ground weft yarn, matching warp, colour A

content.text: for two blocks (A and B) alternates ground threads on shafts 1 and 2 with pattern threads on shafts 3 and 4: Block A = 1, 3, 1, 3 (repeating); Block B = 2, 4, 2, 4 (repeating). A typical unit width is 4 or 6 threads per block, with alternating blocks across the width. Thread the warp at 10 dpi through a 10 dpi reed, two ends per dent.

for summer and winter uses four treadles: Treadle 1 lifts shafts 1+3 (pattern row for Block A); Treadle 2 lifts shafts 1+4 (pattern row for Block B); Treadle 3 lifts shafts 1+3+4 (ground row rising); Treadle 4 lifts shafts 2+3+4 (ground row lowered). The
sequence for a solid block A: T1 (pattern weft) + T3 (ground weft) + T1 (pattern weft) + T4 (ground weft). Repeat. Alternate to block B by substituting T2 for T1 in the pattern rows.
structure works best with a fine warp and a heavier pattern weft. Use a pattern weft one or two weights heavier than the ground warp. This produces a clear, bold block pattern.

items.problem: The blocks show but the pattern does not cover the warp cleanly
items.cause: The pattern weft is too thin relative to the warp sett
items.fix: Switch to a heavier pattern weft, or increase the number of pattern picks per sequence. A pattern weft that is double the weight of the ground weft typically gives clean coverage.

items.problem: The cloth is pulling apart at the block boundaries
items.cause: The threading is not alternating ground and pattern shafts correctly
items.fix: Check the threading sequence at every transition between blocks. Each block boundary should have one thread on the ground shaft before the pattern threads of the next block begin.
